---
title: "ACV Estimator Covariances"
subtitle: "PyApprox Tutorial Library"
description: "Reference: explicit formulas for the Sigma_Delta_Delta and Sigma_Delta_Q0 blocks that determine every ACV estimator's variance, for mean and variance statistics."
tutorial_type: reference
topic: multi_fidelity
difficulty: advanced
estimated_time: 15
prerequisites:
  - acv_many_models_analysis
  - acv_allocation_matrices
tags:
  - multi-fidelity
  - acv
  - covariance-formulas
  - reference
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Download Notebook
[Download as Jupyter Notebook](notebooks/acv_covariances.ipynb)
:::

## Purpose

This is a **reference tutorial**. It derives the explicit formulas for
$\Sigma_{\Delta\Delta}$ and $\Sigma_{\Delta Q_0}$ — the two covariance blocks that
determine every ACV estimator's variance via the Schur complement

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\hat{\mathbf{Q}}_0^{\text{ACV}})
= \Sigma_{Q_0 Q_0}
  - \Sigma_{Q_0\Delta}\,\Sigma_{\Delta\Delta}^{-1}\,\Sigma_{\Delta Q_0}.
$$

The formulas here are used internally by the allocator and `optimized_covariance`.
Read this tutorial when you need to understand how a specific estimator's covariance is
computed, when you are implementing a new statistic type, or when debugging an unexpected
variance prediction.


## Setup and Notation

Let $f_0, \ldots, f_M$ be models. For a statistic $\mathbf{Q}_\alpha$ of model $\alpha$
define:

- $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta} = \mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_\alpha, \mathbf{Q}_\beta)$,
  the **population** covariance between the statistics of models $\alpha$ and $\beta$
- $N_\alpha = |\mathcal{Z}_\alpha|$, the size of sample set $\mathcal{Z}_\alpha$
- $N_{\alpha\beta} = |\mathcal{Z}_\alpha \cap \mathcal{Z}_\beta|$, the overlap size
  (obtained from the allocation matrix and partition sizes as described in
  [ACV Allocation Matrices](acv_allocation_matrices.qmd))
- $\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha = \mathbf{Q}_\alpha(\mathcal{Z}_\alpha^*) - \mathbf{Q}_\alpha(\mathcal{Z}_\alpha)$

The central results below are exact for any estimator that is a sample mean of iid
evaluations. They hold regardless of whether the statistic is the mean, variance,
mean+variance, or any other statistic whose population covariance $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$
can be computed from model pilot samples.


## $\Sigma_{\Delta Q_0}$: Covariance of Corrections with the HF Estimator

The correction $\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha$ uses two sample sets:
$\mathcal{Z}_\alpha^*$ (star) and $\mathcal{Z}_\alpha$ (full). The HF estimator uses
$\mathcal{Z}_0$. The covariance between the HF estimator and correction $\alpha$ is

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \mathbf{Q}_\alpha(\mathcal{Z}_\alpha^*))
  - \mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \mathbf{Q}_\alpha(\mathcal{Z}_\alpha)).
$$

Because sample means over non-overlapping sets are independent, only the shared samples
contribute to each term:

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \mathbf{Q}_\alpha(\mathcal{Z}_\alpha^*))
= \frac{N_{0 \cap \alpha^*}}{N_0 N_{\alpha^*}}\,\Sigma_{0\alpha},
$$

and similarly for the second term with $N_{0 \cap \alpha}$ and $N_\alpha$. Therefore

$$
\boxed{
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \left(\frac{N_{0 \cap \alpha^*}}{N_0 N_{\alpha^*}}
        - \frac{N_{0 \cap \alpha}}{N_0 N_\alpha}\right)\Sigma_{0\alpha}.
}
$$ {#eq-sigma-dq}

The full block is $\Sigma_{\Delta Q_0} = [\Sigma_{\Delta_1 Q_0}^\top, \ldots,
\Sigma_{\Delta_M Q_0}^\top]^\top$ where each row block uses @eq-sigma-dq with the
appropriate intersection sizes.

::: {.callout-note}
## Two-model ACV recovery
For $M = 1$ with $\mathcal{Z}_1^* = \mathcal{Z}_0$ (so $N_{0 \cap 1^*} = N_0$) and
$N_{\alpha^*} = N_0$, @eq-sigma-dq gives
$\frac{1}{N_0}\Sigma_{01} - \frac{N_{0 \cap 1}}{N_0 N_1}\Sigma_{01}$.
For the nested case $\mathcal{Z}_0 \subset \mathcal{Z}_1$, $N_{0 \cap 1} = N_0$, so
this simplifies to $(1/N_0)(1 - N_0/N_1)\Sigma_{01} = \frac{r-1}{r}\frac{C_{01}}{N_0}$,
matching the formula in [ACV Analysis](acv_analysis.qmd). $\checkmark$
:::


## $\Sigma_{\Delta\Delta}$: Covariance Between Corrections

The diagonal blocks (variance of each correction) are

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \left(\frac{1}{N_{\alpha^*}} + \frac{1}{N_\alpha}
        - 2\frac{N_{\alpha^* \cap \alpha}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_\alpha}\right)\Sigma_{\alpha\alpha}.
$$ {#eq-sigma-dd-diag}

For the nested case ($\mathcal{Z}_\alpha^* \subset \mathcal{Z}_\alpha$, so
$N_{\alpha^* \cap \alpha} = N_{\alpha^*}$):

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \left(\frac{1}{N_{\alpha^*}} - \frac{1}{N_\alpha}\right)\Sigma_{\alpha\alpha}.
$$

This matches the MFMC correction variance $\frac{\sigma_\alpha^2}{N_0}(r_{\alpha-1}^{-1} -
r_\alpha^{-1})$ derived in [MFMC Analysis](mfmc_analysis.qmd).

The off-diagonal blocks (covariance between corrections $\alpha$ and $\beta$, $\alpha \neq \beta$):

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\beta)
= \left(
    \frac{N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta^*}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_{\beta^*}}
  - \frac{N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_\beta}
  - \frac{N_{\alpha  \cap \beta^*}}{N_\alpha   N_{\beta^*}}
  + \frac{N_{\alpha  \cap \beta}}{N_\alpha   N_\beta}
  \right)\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}.
$$ {#eq-sigma-dd-offdiag}

When two corrections use completely disjoint star sets and full sets
($N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta^*} = N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta} = N_{\alpha \cap \beta^*} = 0$)
but share the full partition ($N_{\alpha \cap \beta} = N_{0}$, the common HF samples),
@eq-sigma-dd-offdiag simplifies to $\frac{N_0}{N_\alpha N_\beta}\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$.

For MFMC with nested sets, $N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta^*} = N_{\min(\alpha^*, \beta^*)}$
(the smaller of the two star sets), and the expression simplifies to a clean product
of $(r_{\gamma}^{-1} - r_{\gamma+1}^{-1})$ factors — which is why the MFMC variance
decomposition from [MFMC Analysis](mfmc_analysis.qmd) is so clean.


## Numerical Verification

We verify @eq-sigma-dq and @eq-sigma-dd-diag against empirical covariances for the ACVMF
estimator on the polynomial benchmark.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.multifidelity.polynomial_ensemble import (
    polynomial_ensemble_5model,
)
from pyapprox.typing.statest.statistics import MultiOutputMean
from pyapprox.typing.statest import GMFEstimator
from pyapprox.typing.statest.acv import ACVAllocator
from pyapprox.typing.optimization.minimize.scipy.direct import (
    ScipyDirectOptimizer,
)
from pyapprox.typing.optimization.minimize.scipy.trust_constr import (
    ScipyTrustConstrOptimizer,
)
from pyapprox.typing.optimization.minimize.chained.chained_optimizer import (
    ChainedOptimizer,
)

bkd       = NumpyBkd()
benchmark = polynomial_ensemble_5model(bkd)
models    = benchmark.models()
variable  = benchmark.prior()
costs     = benchmark.costs()
nqoi      = models[0].nqoi()
cov_true  = bkd.to_numpy(benchmark.ensemble_covariance())
n_models  = len(models)
M         = n_models - 1

stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi, bkd)
stat.set_pilot_quantities(bkd.asarray(cov_true))

optimizer = ChainedOptimizer(
    ScipyDirectOptimizer(raise_on_failure=False),
    ScipyTrustConstrOptimizer(maxiter=5000),
)

ri_zeros = bkd.zeros(M, dtype=int)
acvmf = GMFEstimator(stat, costs, recursion_index=ri_zeros)
allocator = ACVAllocator(acvmf, optimizer=optimizer)
acvmf.set_allocation(allocator.allocate(100.0))

# Retrieve intersection sizes and partition sizes from the estimator
A = bkd.to_numpy(acvmf._get_allocation_matrix())
p = bkd.to_numpy(acvmf.npartition_samples())

def intersection_size(col_j, col_k, p):
    """Compute |Z_j ∩ Z_k| from allocation matrix columns and partition sizes."""
    return float(np.dot(A[:, col_j] * A[:, col_k], p))

N0 = intersection_size(1, 1, p)   # |Z_0| = col 1 with itself

print("Partition sizes:", [int(x) for x in p])
print(f"N0 = {int(N0)}")

# Compute theoretical Sigma_Delta1_Q0 from eq-sigma-dq
alpha = 1   # first LF model
# col 2 = Z_1*, col 1 = Z_0, col 3 = Z_1
N_1st      = intersection_size(2, 2, p)  # |Z_1*|
N_0cap1st  = intersection_size(1, 2, p)  # |Z_0 ∩ Z_1*|
N_1        = intersection_size(3, 3, p)  # |Z_1|
N_0cap1    = intersection_size(1, 3, p)  # |Z_0 ∩ Z_1|

Sigma_01 = cov_true[0, 1]  # population Cov(f_0, f_1)
theory_cov_d1_q0 = (N_0cap1st / (N0 * N_1st) - N_0cap1 / (N0 * N_1)) * Sigma_01

print(f"\nFor alpha=1 (f_1):")
print(f"  N_1*={int(N_1st)}, N_0∩Z_1*={int(N_0cap1st)}, "
      f"N_1={int(N_1)}, N_0∩Z_1={int(N_0cap1)}")
print(f"  Theoretical Cov(Delta_1, Q_0) = {theory_cov_d1_q0:.6f}")
```

```{python}
# Verify empirically by running many trials
n_trials = 5000
q0_vals    = np.empty(n_trials)
delta1_vals = np.empty(n_trials)

for i in range(n_trials):
    np.random.seed(i + 7000)
    spm = acvmf.generate_samples_per_model(lambda n: variable.rvs(int(n)))
    # Q_0(Z_0): mean of f_0 over the HF partition
    q0_vals[i] = float(bkd.mean(models[0](spm[0])[0]))
    # Delta_1 = Q_1(Z_1*) - Q_1(Z_1)
    # Z_1* is the first N_1* samples of spm[1]; Z_1 is all of spm[1]
    N1st = int(N_1st)
    v1 = bkd.to_numpy(models[1](spm[1])).ravel()
    delta1_vals[i] = v1[:N1st].mean() - v1.mean()

empirical_cov = np.cov(q0_vals, delta1_vals)[0, 1]
print(f"Empirical  Cov(Delta_1, Q_0) = {empirical_cov:.6f}")
print(f"Theoretical                  = {theory_cov_d1_q0:.6f}")
print(f"Relative error: {abs(empirical_cov - theory_cov_d1_q0) / abs(theory_cov_d1_q0):.3f}")
```


## Extension to Other Statistics

The formulas @eq-sigma-dq and @eq-sigma-dd-offdiag depend only on the **population
covariance** $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ between the statistics of models $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
The same structural formulas apply to any statistic, with $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$
replaced by the population covariance of the relevant statistic.

For the **variance** statistic, $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ involves fourth-order moments
(the covariance of variance estimators). For the **mean-and-variance** estimator
(a two-dimensional statistic per model), $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ is a $2 \times 2$ block
involving both second and fourth-order moments.

PyApprox computes the appropriate $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ from pilot samples via
`stat.compute_pilot_quantities(values_per_model)`. The rest of the machinery —
@eq-sigma-dq, @eq-sigma-dd-diag, @eq-sigma-dd-offdiag — is the same regardless of
which statistic is being estimated.

```{python}
# Demonstrate: switching from mean to variance estimation changes Sigma_alpha_beta
# but uses the same allocation machinery

from pyapprox.typing.statest.statistics import MultiOutputVariance

stat_var = MultiOutputVariance(nqoi, bkd)

N_pilot = 500
samples_pilot = variable.rvs(N_pilot)
vals_pilot = [m(samples_pilot) for m in models]
pilot_quantities_var = stat_var.compute_pilot_quantities(vals_pilot)
stat_var.set_pilot_quantities(*pilot_quantities_var)

acvmf_var = GMFEstimator(stat_var, costs, recursion_index=ri_zeros)
allocator_var = ACVAllocator(acvmf_var, optimizer=optimizer)
acvmf_var.set_allocation(allocator_var.allocate(100.0))

print("ACVMF for mean:     predicted std =",
      float(acvmf.optimized_covariance()[0, 0])**0.5)
print("ACVMF for variance: predicted std =",
      float(acvmf_var.optimized_covariance()[0, 0])**0.5)
print("\nAllocation (mean):",    [int(x) for x in bkd.to_numpy(acvmf.npartition_samples())])
print("Allocation (variance):", [int(x) for x in bkd.to_numpy(acvmf_var.npartition_samples())])
```

The allocation matrix structure (which partitions are in which sets) is identical for
both statistics. What changes is the population covariance $\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ —
and therefore the optimal partition sizes $\mathbf{p}^*$.


## Key Formulas Summary

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\mathbf{Q}_0(\mathcal{Z}_0),\, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \left(\frac{N_{0 \cap \alpha^*}}{N_0 N_{\alpha^*}}
        - \frac{N_{0 \cap \alpha}}{N_0 N_\alpha}\right)\Sigma_{0\alpha}
\tag{\ref{eq-sigma-dq}}
$$

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha)
= \left(\frac{1}{N_{\alpha^*}} + \frac{1}{N_\alpha}
        - \frac{2 N_{\alpha^* \cap \alpha}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_\alpha}\right)\Sigma_{\alpha\alpha}
\tag{\ref{eq-sigma-dd-diag}}
$$

$$
\mathbb{C}\mathrm{ov}(\boldsymbol{\Delta}_\alpha, \boldsymbol{\Delta}_\beta)
= \left(
    \frac{N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta^*}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_{\beta^*}}
  - \frac{N_{\alpha^* \cap \beta}}{N_{\alpha^*} N_\beta}
  - \frac{N_{\alpha  \cap \beta^*}}{N_\alpha   N_{\beta^*}}
  + \frac{N_{\alpha  \cap \beta}}{N_\alpha   N_\beta}
  \right)\Sigma_{\alpha\beta}
\tag{\ref{eq-sigma-dd-offdiag}}
$$

All intersection sizes are computed from the allocation matrix via
$|\mathcal{Z}_j \cap \mathcal{Z}_k| = \mathbf{a}_j^\top \mathrm{diag}(\mathbf{p})\,\mathbf{a}_k$.


## References

- [GGEJJCP2020] A. Gorodetsky, S. Geraci, M. Eldred, J. Jakeman. *A generalized
  approximate control variate framework for multifidelity uncertainty quantification.*
  Journal of Computational Physics, 408:109257, 2020.
  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcp.2020.109257)
